historical_evidence.reason: The eight Phase 5 simulations are prior regression evidence and are not executions in this campaign.

black-box-pilot.description: A fresh Codex-operated clean-state product simulation using only public product information, a persona, and a task.

automated-regression.description: Deterministic unit, integration, browser, accessibility, security, or build automation.

package-lifecycle-smoke.description: Machine-collected package startup, capability, preservation, shutdown, or platform assurance.

operator-receipt.description: Hash-bound machine or operator evidence that is not an independent black-box session.

challenge-review.description: A fresh adversarial review of coverage, evidence, defects, or release claims.

human-research.description: Reserved for separately recruited external participants; this campaign contains no human-research evidence.

clean_state_requirements: isolated npm cache and temporary install location
fresh browser profile or cleared storage
isolated project root containing no user project
bounded environment-variable baseline with secrets excluded from evidence
no reused local session or pairing value

hosted-browser: isolated npm cache and temporary install location
isolated project root containing no user project
bounded environment-variable baseline with secrets excluded from evidence

severity_crosswalk.P3: Low but reproducible or materially contrary to the product contract
severity_crosswalk.enhancement: Non-defect improvement that does not contradict an existing promise

revalidation_triggers: package bytes, inventory, version, or provenance changes
static artifact or production release changes
validation policy or security contract changes
supported operating system, architecture, Node, or browser claim changes
DuskDS public node evidence exceeds 15 hours
TLS, monitoring, or rollback evidence exceeds its policy window

pilots.persona: First-time Windows developer who has never built on Dusk
pilots.task: Use the production website to choose DuskDS, complete the manual hosted journey as far as honestly possible, recover from one failed prerequisite, and explain the next real-world action.

pilots.persona: Experienced Windows and WSL DuskDS developer
pilots.task: Install the public package in Local Actions mode and complete the entire DuskDS Setup to Access to Build to deployment-readiness to Inspect journey through the UI, using an isolated project and real evidence.

pilots.persona: Security-conscious novice local user
pilots.task: Start in Safe mode, determine its limits, intentionally attempt one unavailable machine action, transition to Local Actions, complete the intended action, and shut down cleanly.

pilots.persona: CLI-oriented Windows developer
pilots.task: Discover and use the direct create-duskds command in an empty isolated directory, inspect the created project, confirm preservation after shutdown, and clean up.

pilots.persona: Experienced developer protecting an existing repository
pilots.task: Use the existing-project journey without granting Studio write access, record valid manual evidence, navigate recovery help, and confirm the repository remains unchanged.

pilots.persona: Linux-native DuskDS developer
pilots.task: From a clean Ubuntu environment, run the public package, create the reviewed starter, execute the supported build and VM-test lane, record evidence, shut down, and verify cleanup.

pilots.persona: macOS arm64 developer evaluating DuskDS
pilots.task: Run Safe and Local Actions from the public package, complete supported checks and starter creation, verify the native VM-test limitation is truthful, and shut down without residue.

pilots.persona: Solidity developer evaluating DuskEVM before Testnet launch
pilots.task: Use the production website to understand DuskEVM, inspect all available pre-launch material, classify identifiers, seek wallet and deployment actions, and determine exactly what is and is not live.

pilots.persona: First-time npm user on supported Windows
pilots.task: With an empty npm cache, install and start the exact public package, identify the version and release source in the UI, then stop and remove all temporary state.

pilots.persona: Supply-chain-conscious JavaScript developer
pilots.task: Independently obtain the public package, verify its registry integrity, provenance, inventory, commit and displayed release identity, then launch only if those facts agree.

pilots.persona: Developer repeating an installation after deleting local caches
pilots.task: Run the package twice from separately isolated npm caches and browser profiles, compare behavior and identity, then confirm both sessions and ports are gone.

pilots.persona: Developer with an older unsupported Node 24 installation
pilots.task: Attempt first run below the supported Node boundary, assess the refusal and recovery guidance, install or select the supported version, and retry successfully.

pilots.persona: Release engineer checking Node compatibility boundaries
pilots.task: Exercise the exact minimum supported Node release and an above-range Node release in isolation, verify success and refusal behavior respectively, and leave no services running.

pilots.persona: Existing user moving from the previous public package version
pilots.task: Run the previous public version in isolation, stop it, move to the current version without reusing sessions, verify project preservation and release identity, then reinstall the current version once more.

pilots.persona: Keyboard-only developer using Chrome
pilots.task: Navigate every production route and all primary controls without a pointer, including empty-result and destructive-confirmation recovery, while checking focus order and announcements.

pilots.persona: Firefox developer unfamiliar with the Studio
pilots.task: Complete path selection, manual DuskDS Setup and Access, reference search, troubleshooting, external-doc return, and browser-data reset using Firefox.

pilots.persona: Safari-oriented developer using the WebKit compatibility run
pilots.task: Exercise all routes, DuskEVM pre-launch behavior, copy controls, forms, disclosures and reset behavior in WebKit, explicitly labelling the result as engine emulation rather than real Safari.

pilots.persona: Developer using a narrow touch device
pilots.task: Inspect and operate every route at 320, 390, 760 and 761 CSS-pixel widths, checking reflow, overflow, touch targets, sticky controls and long commands.

pilots.persona: Low-vision developer using desktop zoom
pilots.task: Operate the full shell and long DuskDS pages at 1120, 1121, 1280 and 1440 widths and at actual 200 percent browser zoom, checking clipping, order, focus visibility and readability.

pilots.persona: Developer using high contrast, reduced motion and assistive technology
pilots.task: Traverse every route with forced colors and reduced motion, inspect the accessibility tree, run route-wide WCAG checks, and verify names, states, status announcements and target sizes.

pilots.persona: Developer whose usual frontend port is already occupied
pilots.task: Start the public package while 127.0.0.1 port 5173 is occupied, assess the refusal, release the port, recover successfully, and confirm independent cleanup.

pilots.persona: Developer whose local companion port is already occupied
pilots.task: Start the public package while 127.0.0.1 port 8788 is occupied, assess partial-start cleanup and guidance, release the port, recover, and confirm no orphan process.

pilots.persona: Developer on an unstable or offline network
pilots.task: Use hosted and local DuskDS Access while the node request is unavailable, observe timeout and error states, restore networking, retry, and verify that no false evidence was recorded.

pilots.persona: Developer who interrupts long-running local work
pilots.task: Interrupt startup, preflight and starter creation at meaningful points, then verify descendant cleanup, transactional filesystem behavior, project preservation, recovery and port closure.

pilots.persona: Developer with two tabs or launches and a stale local session
pilots.task: Create concurrent local launches and browser sessions, expire or invalidate one session, attempt overlapping starter operations, recover safely, and confirm one truthful final state.

pilots.persona: Developer with incomplete tools and unusual filesystem conditions
pilots.task: Exercise missing and incompatible tools, a Unicode project name or parent where supported, and a read-only target, then follow the product recovery guidance without creating partial or misleading evidence.

pilots.persona: Web security engineer testing local authentication
pilots.task: Treat the running local Studio as a black box and attempt hostile Host, Origin, CORS, Private Network Access, bootstrap, pairing, cookie reuse and stale-session requests without obtaining unauthorized capability.

pilots.persona: Filesystem security engineer
pilots.task: Attempt traversal, absolute/root, link, junction or reparse, reserved-name, unsafe-character and boundary-length project locations against Local Actions and direct CLI using disposable fixtures only.

pilots.persona: Developer protecting existing and read-only data
pilots.task: Attempt creation over existing files, directories, read-only targets and concurrent targets, then verify no overwrite, merge, outside-root write, partial promotion or user-project mutation occurred.

pilots.persona: Application security tester sending hostile inputs
pilots.task: Probe malformed JSON, oversized bodies, slow or repeated requests, unsupported methods, hostile identifiers and concurrency limits, then confirm bounded failures and continued healthy operation.

pilots.persona: Security engineer checking diagnostics and child environments
pilots.task: Seed disposable secret-shaped environment values and hostile local outputs, run allowed checks and diagnostics, and verify secrets, paths, terminal output and file contents are not exposed or persisted.

pilots.persona: Endpoint security engineer testing process containment
pilots.task: Cause an allowed tool fixture to create descendant or detached work, trigger timeout and interruption, and independently verify process-tree termination, closed ports, bounded output and filesystem cleanup.
